title: How to Choose a Career When You Have No Idea What You Want
author: Tklesson Team
content:
You're supposed to choose a career path, but you don't even know what you want for breakfast tomorrow. This is normal. Here's how to figure it out without panicking.

You Don't Need to Know Everything at 18

The average person changes careers 3-7 times in their life. The job you do at 25 probably won't exist by the time you're 40. Your first choice doesn't lock you in forever. Stop treating this like a life-or-death decision. It's not.

What you need right now: direction, not a destination. A compass, not a GPS.

Start with What You Don't Want

It's often easier to identify what you hate than what you love. Make a list:
• Do you hate sitting at a desk all day?
• Do you hate public speaking?
• Do you hate repetitive tasks?
• Do you hate high-pressure environments?
• Do you hate working alone?

Eliminate careers that require what you hate. This narrows the field significantly.

Identify Your Energy Activities

Pay attention to what leaves you energized vs drained. After studying math for three hours, do you feel accomplished or exhausted? After helping a friend solve a problem, do you feel satisfied or annoyed? These feelings are clues.

Your career will involve doing similar tasks for 40+ hours a week. Choose something that doesn't leave you depleted.

The Three Circles Framework

Draw three overlapping circles:
Circle 1: What you're good at (skills, talents, subjects you excel in)
Circle 2: What you enjoy (activities that make time disappear)
Circle 3: What the world needs (jobs that pay and have demand)

Your ideal career is in the intersection of all three. If it's only in circles 1 and 2, it's a hobby. Only in 1 and 3, you'll be bored. Only in 2 and 3, you'll struggle without skills.

Conduct Informational Interviews (This Is the Most Useful Thing You Can Do)

Talk to people who actually have the jobs you're considering. Ask them:
• What does a typical day look like?
• What do you wish you knew before starting?
• What's the worst part of the job?
• What skills matter most?
• Would you choose this career again?

One conversation is worth 100 hours of internet research. People love talking about themselves. Just ask.

Test Drive Before You Commit

Shadow someone for a day. Volunteer. Take an introductory class. Do a small project. You don't need to commit to a career to try it out. Sample first, then decide.

College is an expensive way to discover you hate something. Discover before you pay.

Separate Interest from Identity

'I want to be a doctor' is different from 'I'm interested in medicine.' The first is an identity; the second is an exploration. Keep exploration open. You can be interested in law without becoming a lawyer. You can love art without majoring in it.

Your career is what you do, not who you are.

The Best Career Advice No One Gives

Choose your boss, not your job title. A great manager at an okay company is better than a terrible manager at a great company. Your immediate supervisor affects your daily happiness more than your salary or prestige.

Also: choose your location. Where you live shapes your lifestyle, relationships, and opportunities. A dream job in a city you hate won't feel like a dream.

It's Okay to Change Your Mind

The most successful people didn't follow a straight line. They tried things, failed, learned, and pivoted. You're allowed to do the same. Your major is not your destiny. Your first job is not your life sentence.

Start somewhere. Adjust along the way. That's how everyone figures it out.
